---
title: "Resource Economics and Token Budgets"
chapter_id: "resource-economics-and-token-budgets"
part_id: "routing-compression-representation-substrates"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "tokenmana"
  - "planforge"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "simulation_scaling"
  - "viea"
  - "project_theseus_whitepaper"
  - "coilra_multicoil_rope"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Resource Economics and Token Budgets

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `resource-economics-and-token-budgets` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `tokenmana`, `planforge`, `coherence_exchange`, `simulation_scaling`, `viea`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `coilra_multicoil_rope` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `tokenmana`, `planforge`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `simulation_scaling`, `viea`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `coilra_multicoil_rope` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `planforge`, `coherence_exchange`, `viea`; raw cache: `tokenmana`, `planforge`, `simulation_scaling`, `viea`; pending source notes: `tokenmana`, `simulation_scaling`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `coilra_multicoil_rope`; connector/recovery: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Compute, context, verification, and human attention are scarce resources that the architecture must allocate explicitly.

Within the ASI Stack, this is a capability substrate layer problem. The layer receives tasks, residuals, benchmarks, specialist capabilities, resource budgets, representation constraints, and mathematical contracts and must turn them into routes, compressed artifacts, specialist modules, recurrence contracts, benchmark frontiers, and residual records. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Semantic Representation and Tree-Structured Models, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It hands its output to Simulation Fidelity and Physical Constraints, where the next layer tightens or implements the same constraint. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Ignoring resource economics makes high-quality verification unaffordable and encourages synchronized overload or hidden cost shifts.

In this chapter, the failure is more specific than bad prompting or weak model choice: ignoring resource economics makes high-quality verification unaffordable and encourages synchronized overload or hidden cost shifts. If `resource-economics-and-token-budgets` is left implicit, downstream layers inherit a capability whose authority, evidence burden, and rollback conditions are unclear. The stack therefore treats the missing contract as the defect, not merely the local model behavior.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[resource-economics-and-token-budgets.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The stack should account for token budgets, verification tax, load stability, and risk-adjusted inference value.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - TokenMana, PlanForge, The Coherence Exchange, Simulation Scaling Law, and Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, track task value, uncertainty, cost of error, and inference cost. Second, use regenerative or budgeted capacity mechanisms where useful. Third, escalate verification when risk justifies the cost.

- Track task value, uncertainty, cost of error, and inference cost.
- Use regenerative or budgeted capacity mechanisms where useful.
- Escalate verification when risk justifies the cost.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

The interface for Resource Economics and Token Budgets is the contract that lets adjacent layers compose without absorbing each other's authority. It should make the chapter's output legible to planning, memory, reasoning, execution, evidence, and improvement layers while preserving the boundary described by the core claim.

- Planning allocates budgets.
- Routing chooses costed specialists.
- Evidence measures cost-quality tradeoffs.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: tasks, residuals, benchmarks, specialist capabilities, resource budgets, representation constraints, and mathematical contracts. The downstream interface emits routes, compressed artifacts, specialist modules, recurrence contracts, benchmark frontiers, and residual records. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Budgets do not override protected safety gates.
- High-risk tasks pay verification cost.
- Cost savings are recorded with quality results.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Cost-cutting verification away.
- Load-synchronized degradation.
- Resource hoarding by low-value tasks.

For Resource Economics and Token Budgets, mitigation starts by turning each listed failure into a named reviewable artifact. When `Cost-cutting verification away` appears, the system should emit a residual, rejection, quarantine, regression, or human-review record rather than allowing the failure to disappear into later fluent output.

## Minimal implementation

A resource ledger with budget, risk, cost, quality, and verification tax fields.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: A resource ledger with budget, risk, cost, quality, and verification tax fields. It should be easy to validate, easy to render in the book, and easy for a later coding run to convert into a schema, Lean predicate, deterministic fixture, trace table, or replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Budget allocation test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Resource Economics and Token Budgets boundary. | planned; not run |
| Risk-adjusted verification test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Resource Economics and Token Budgets boundary. | planned; not run |
| Load stability scenario | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Resource Economics and Token Budgets boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:resources.budgets.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ResourceEconomics` | A task budget cannot disable required safety or verification gates. | planned |
| `lean:resources.budgets.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ResourceEconomics` | A high-risk task with insufficient verification budget is blocked or escalated. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `tokenmana` | TokenMana | resource_economics | Regenerative capacity mechanisms for load-stable token pricing. | local raw cache available |
| `planforge` | PlanForge | planning_control | Planning substrate. Goal-to-execution compilation, hierarchical decomposition, DAG planning, scheduling, intelligence arbitrage. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `simulation_scaling` | Simulation Scaling Law | compute_fidelity_constraints | Resource constraints on scope, clockspeed, and fidelity in simulations. | local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `project_theseus_whitepaper` | Project Theseus Whitepaper | report_first_rmi_prototype | Local-first report-driven RMI implementation reference: SymLiquid, SparkStream, Octopus Router, residual escrow, self-evolution gates, Hive runtime, observability. | source note pending |
| `coilra_multicoil_rope` | CoilRA and MultiCoil RoPE | cyclic_mixers_position_encoding | Adapter-block, residue/winding, block-cyclic, multicoil, relative RoPE, circulant convolution, cyclic mixer, and parameter-accounting substrate with explicit non-claims. | source note pending |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Resource Economics and Token Budgets defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: The stack should account for token budgets, verification tax, load stability, and risk-adjusted inference value. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

In v0.2, this chapter gives Resource Economics and Token Budgets a complete architectural boundary: compute, context, verification, and human attention are scarce resources that the architecture must allocate explicitly. Its next maturity step is to substantiate the boundary through `Planning allocates budgets`, preserve `Budgets do not override protected safety gates`, and replace planned proof/test hooks with validated artifacts. Claims should move only when source notes, code, proofs, or run records justify the movement.
